Pearson Edexcel International GCSE
Mark Scheme
Summer 2026 · Predicted Paper (Moderate)
In Economics (4EC1)
Paper 2: Macroeconomics and the Global Economy


General Marking Guidance
   •   All candidates must receive the same treatment. Examiners must mark the first candidate in
       exactly the same way as they mark the last.
   •   Mark schemes should be applied positively. Candidates must be rewarded for what they
       have shown they can do rather than penalised for omissions.
   •   Examiners should mark according to the mark scheme not according to their perception of
       where the grade boundaries may lie.
   •   There is no ceiling on achievement. All marks on the mark scheme should be used
       appropriately.
   •   All the marks on the mark scheme are designed to be awarded. Examiners should always
       award full marks if deserved.
   •   Where some judgement is required, mark schemes will provide the principles by which
       marks will be awarded and exemplification may be limited.
   •   When examiners are in doubt regarding the application of the mark scheme to a candidate's
       response, the team leader must be consulted.
   •   Crossed out work should be marked UNLESS the candidate has replaced it with an
       alternative response.
Mark Scheme: Paper 2 · Macroeconomics and the Global Economy (4EC1/02) · Predicted
(Moderate)

 1(a)      Question                                                                (1)
           Which one of the following is a benefit of economic growth?
           Answer / Indicative Content
                                     AO1 1 mark
           The only correct answer is:
           B Higher living standards

           A is not correct because higher unemployment is a sign of weak, not
           strong, growth.
           C is not correct because economic growth typically raises tax
           revenue.
           D is not correct because exports often rise when an economy grows.


 1(b)      Question                                                                (1)
           Which one of the following is an example of a leakage from the
           circular flow of income?
           Answer / Indicative Content
                                     AO1 1 mark
           The only correct answer is:
           C Household savings

           A is not correct because consumer spending is a flow within the
           economy.
           B is not correct because investment is an injection into the circular
           flow.
           D is not correct because government spending is an injection into
           the circular flow.


 1(c)      Question                                                                (2)
           What is meant by the term inflation?
           Answer / Indicative Content
                                    AO1 2 marks
           Award up to 2 marks for a correct definition:
           A sustained/continuous rise in the general price level (1) in an
           economy over a period of time (1)

           OR

           The rate at which the average level of prices is rising (1) causing a
           fall in the purchasing power of money (1)

           Accept any other appropriate response


 1(d)      Question                                                                (2)
           Describe one impact an ageing population may have on an
       economy.
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                AO1 2 marks
       Award 1 mark for identification of an impact and 1 mark for
       development.

       One impact is increased government spending on healthcare and
       pensions (1) as elderly people generally require more medical care
       and receive state pensions (1)

       OR

       One impact is a shrinking labour force (1) as more people retire than
       enter the workforce, reducing the country's potential output (1)

       Accept any other appropriate response


1(e)   Question                                                                (2)
       Calculate the value of Vietnam's GDP in $bn in 2023. (Show
       working.)
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                AO2 2 marks
       Award 1 mark for correct method (C + I + G + X − M):
       280 + 85 + 55 + 320 − 300 (1)

       Award 1 mark for correct final answer:
       = $440bn (1)

       Award 2 marks if the correct answer of $440bn is given even without
       working shown.
       Award 1 mark only if $bn units are missing (e.g. just 440).
       Do not award marks for the formula alone.


1(f)   Question                                                                (3)
       Draw the effects of the subsidy on rice in Vietnam.
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                AO2 3 marks
       Award 1 mark for a rightward (outward) shift of the supply curve,
       labelled S1.
       Award 1 mark for a lower new equilibrium price, labelled (e.g. P1).
       Award 1 mark for a higher new equilibrium quantity, labelled (e.g.
       Q1).


1(g)   Question                                                                (3)
       Explain one reason why unemployment may fall during economic
       growth.
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                      AO2 3 marks
          Award 1 mark for identification of a relevant reason.
          Award 1 mark for development.
          Award 1 mark for response in context (e.g. Vietnam).

          One reason is that higher economic growth leads to higher demand
          for goods and services (1). In response, firms in Vietnam need to
          hire more workers to increase production (1), which reduces
          unemployment from 2.3% to an even lower rate (1).

          Accept any other appropriate response.


1(h)      Question                                                                      (6)
          With reference to the data and your knowledge of economics,
          analyse the benefits to consumers in Vietnam of being a member of
          ASEAN.
          Indicative Content · AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks)

          AO2 and AO3
            • A trading bloc is a group of countries that reduces trade
                barriers such as tariffs and quotas between members.
              •   Vietnamese consumers have access to a wider variety of
                  goods from 10 ASEAN countries · products that may not be
                  produced at home, such as specialist Thai, Indonesian or
                  Singaporean goods.
              •   Reduced tariffs mean that imports from other ASEAN
                  members are cheaper, lowering prices for Vietnamese
                  consumers on items such as electronics, food and clothing.
              •   Competition from 680 million people's worth of producers
                  encourages domestic Vietnamese firms to improve quality
                  and keep prices low.
              •   Lower prices increase real incomes/purchasing power of
                  Vietnamese households, raising living standards.
              •   Access to better technology and innovation through imports
                  (e.g. smartphones, vehicles) improves consumer welfare.


Level     Mark        Descriptor

          0               •   No rewardable material.

Level 1   1–2             •   Demonstrates basic knowledge and understanding by
                              developing relevant points. Limited application of economic
                              terms, concepts, theories and calculations (AO2).
                          •   Information presented will lack selectivity and organisation.
                              Interpretation of economic information will be limited, with a
                              lack of analysis of issues (AO3).

Level 2   3–4             •   Demonstrates partial knowledge and understanding by
                              developing relevant points. Partial application of economic
                              terms, concepts, theories and calculations (AO2).
Level     Mark   Descriptor

                    •   Information presented will demonstrate some selectivity and
                        organisation. Interpretation of economic information will be
                        good, with some analysis of issues (AO3).

Level 3   5–6       •   Demonstrates clear knowledge and understanding by
                        developing relevant points. Appropriate application of
                        economic terms, concepts, theories and calculations (AO2).
                    •   Information presented will demonstrate excellent selectivity
                        and organisation. Interpretation of economic information will
                        be excellent, with a thorough analysis of issues (AO3).
2(a)   Question                                                                  (1)
       Which one of the following is an example of a progressive tax?
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                 AO1 1 mark
       The only correct answer is:
       C Income tax rates that rise as income rises

       A is not correct because a flat sales tax takes the same rate from all,
       not a higher rate from higher earners.
       B is not correct because a fixed excise duty is an indirect tax, not
       progressive.
       D is not correct because a fixed amount per household is regressive
       · it forms a larger share of low incomes.


2(b)   Question                                                                  (1)
       Which one of the following is the best description of structural
       unemployment?
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                 AO1 1 mark
       The only correct answer is:
       B Workers whose skills no longer match available jobs

       A is not correct because this describes frictional unemployment.
       C is not correct because this describes cyclical unemployment.
       D is not correct because this describes seasonal unemployment.


2(c)   Question                                                                  (1)
       State one possible impact of a high rate of inflation on consumers.
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                 AO1 1 mark
       Award 1 mark for any one impact:
         • Reduces the purchasing power/real value of money
          •   Fall in real incomes if wages do not rise as fast as prices
          •   Falling standard of living
          •   Reduces the real value of savings
          •   Uncertainty about future prices discouraging spending


       Accept any other appropriate response


2(d)   Question                                                                  (2)
       What is meant by the term direct tax?
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                AO1 2 marks
       Award up to 2 marks for a correct definition:
       A tax paid directly to the government (1) on the income, wealth or
       profits of individuals or firms (1)
       OR

       A tax on the income of individuals or profits of firms (1) where the
       burden of the tax cannot be passed on to anyone else (1)

       Accept any other appropriate response


2(e)   Question                                                                  (3)
       Explain one way increased government spending can reduce
       unemployment in a country such as Brazil.
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                AO2 3 marks
       Award 1 mark for identification of a relevant way.
       Award 1 mark for development.
       Award 1 mark for response in the Brazilian context.

       One way is that government infrastructure spending directly creates
       jobs in the construction industry (1). Building roads, schools and
       hospitals requires labour such as construction workers, engineers
       and architects (1). With a 45% increase in infrastructure spending in
       Brazil, many previously unemployed workers can be hired to
       complete these projects (1).

       Accept any other appropriate response.


2(f)   Question                                                                  (3)
       Label the remaining three stages of the economic cycle.
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                AO2 3 marks
       Award 1 mark for correctly labelling Boom (at the peak of the curve).
       Award 1 mark for correctly labelling Downturn (or Slowdown,
       between peak and trough).
       Award 1 mark for correctly labelling Recovery (rising portion after the
       trough).


2(g)   Question                                                                  (9)
       Assess the possible benefits for Brazil of reducing income tax rates.
       Indicative Content · AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks) / AO4 (3
       marks)

       AO2 and AO3 · benefits
         • Income tax is a direct tax on individuals' earnings;
             progressive rates apply higher percentages to higher
             incomes.
            •   Lower income tax rates give workers more disposable
                income, which boosts consumer spending in Brazil.
            •   Higher consumer spending can stimulate aggregate demand,
                leading to higher economic growth.
              •   Lower income tax may act as an incentive to work longer
                  hours or enter the labour force, increasing productivity.
              •   Reducing tax rates can encourage skilled workers and
                  entrepreneurs to remain in Brazil rather than emigrate ·
                  reducing 'brain drain'.
              •   Lower taxes may reduce tax avoidance and the
                  informal/black economy, ultimately raising overall tax revenue
                  in the long run.


          AO4 · drawbacks / evaluation
            • A tax cut reduces government revenue in the short term,
               potentially increasing Brazil's fiscal deficit.
              •   Reduced revenue may force cuts in public services such as
                  healthcare, education or infrastructure, hurting low-income
                  groups.
              •   Lower income tax may widen the gap between rich and poor
                  if the wealthy benefit more than the poor, increasing
                  inequality.
              •   Higher spending could cause demand-pull inflation if the
                  economy is near full capacity.
              •   The effectiveness depends on whether consumers spend or
                  save the extra income · wealthy households tend to save
                  more.
              •   Overall judgement: the benefits depend on the size of the cut,
                  the state of the economy, and who benefits most from the
                  cut.


Level     Mark        Descriptor

          0               •   No rewardable material.

Level 1   1–3             •   Demonstrates basic knowledge and understanding by
                              developing relevant points. Limited application of economic
                              terms, concepts, theories and calculations (AO2).
                          •   Information presented will lack selectivity and organisation.
                              Interpretation of economic information will be limited, with a
                              lack of analysis of issues (AO3).
                          •   Only offers one viewpoint, meaning there is an unbalanced
                              and incomplete evaluation, showing limited understanding
                              and awareness (AO4).

Level 2   4–6             •   Demonstrates partial knowledge and understanding by
                              developing relevant points. Partial application of economic
                              terms, concepts, theories and calculations (AO2).
                          •   Information presented will demonstrate some selectivity and
                              organisation. Interpretation of economic information will be
                              good, with some analysis of issues (AO3).
                          •   Offers more than one viewpoint but the argument may lack
                              balance, leading to an evaluation that may not demonstrate
                              full understanding and awareness (AO4).
Level     Mark   Descriptor

Level 3   7–9       •   Demonstrates clear knowledge and understanding by
                        developing relevant points. Appropriate application of
                        economic terms, concepts, theories and calculations (AO2).
                    •   Information presented will demonstrate excellent selectivity
                        and organisation. Interpretation of economic information will
                        be excellent, with a thorough analysis of issues (AO3).
                    •   Offers more than one viewpoint. The argument is well
                        balanced and coherent, leading to an evaluation that
                        demonstrates full understanding and awareness (AO4).
3(a)   Question                                                                (1)
       Which one of the following is an example of foreign direct investment
       (FDI)?
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                 AO2 1 mark
       The only correct answer is:
       B A German car company building a factory in India

       A is not correct because this is an example of tourism/consumer
       spending abroad.
       C is not correct because this is an example of importing goods.
       D is not correct because this is an example of exporting goods.


3(b)   Question                                                                (1)
       How many US dollars ($) would ₹8,300 be exchanged for (at $1 =
       ₹83)?
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                 AO2 1 mark
       The only correct answer is:
       B $100

       A is not correct because this is ₹8,300 ÷ 830.
       C is not correct because this is ₹8,300 ÷ 8.3.
       D is not correct because this is ₹8,300 × 10.

       Method: 8,300 ÷ 83 = $100.


3(c)   Question                                                                (3)
       Draw the effects of a Kenyan shilling depreciation on Kenyan tea
       sold abroad.
       Answer / Indicative Content
                                AO2 3 marks
       A depreciation makes Kenyan tea cheaper to foreign buyers, raising
       demand.

       Award 1 mark for a rightward shift of the demand curve, labelled D1.
       Award 1 mark for a higher new equilibrium price, labelled (e.g. P1).
       Award 1 mark for a higher new equilibrium quantity, labelled (e.g.
       Q1).


3(d)   Question                                                                (6)
       Analyse the possible benefits of globalisation for consumers in a
       country such as India.
       Indicative Content · AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks)

       AO2 and AO3
         • Globalisation is the increasing interconnectedness of world
             economies through trade, investment and technology.
              •   Indian consumers can access a wider variety of goods ·
                  from imported electronics and cars to foreign food and
                  fashion · that may not be produced domestically.
              •   Competition from foreign producers and economies of scale
                  have driven down prices on many goods, such as
                  smartphones, clothing and household items.
              •   Record global trade of $33 trillion reflects consumers
                  benefiting from cheaper imports.
              •   Increased choice and lower prices raise real incomes and
                  living standards.
              •   Technology transfer and innovation from global firms (e.g. in
                  IT and online services) benefit Indian consumers.

3(e)      Question                                                                      (9)
          Assess how an increase in import tariffs may affect consumers in a
          country such as Kenya.
          Indicative Content · AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks) / AO4 (3
          marks)

          AO2 and AO3 · negative effects on consumers
            • A tariff is a tax on imported goods, raising their domestic
                price.
              •   With sugar tariffs rising from 35% to 50%, imported sugar
                  becomes more expensive in Kenya.
              •   Consumers face higher prices for sugar and for sugar-based
                  products such as soft drinks, baked goods and confectionery.
              •   The rise in prices reduces real incomes and purchasing
                  power of Kenyan consumers.
              •   Consumer choice is reduced as some foreign suppliers may
                  exit the Kenyan market altogether.
              •   Tariffs can act like a regressive tax, hurting low-income
                  households most because they spend a higher share of
                  income on food.


          AO4 · counter-points / evaluation
            • Tariffs may protect Kenyan domestic sugar producers,
               keeping jobs in sugar farming · indirectly benefitting some
               consumers who are workers.
              •   Government earns tariff revenue which could fund services
                  that benefit consumers (e.g. schools, hospitals).
              •   Domestic production may increase, so consumers may still
                  access sugar at competitive prices from local producers.
              •   The overall impact depends on the price elasticity of demand
                  for sugar and the availability of domestic substitutes.
              •   Overall judgement: in the short run the tariff is likely to raise
                  prices and hurt consumers; in the long run it depends on
                  whether domestic producers can expand to meet demand.

4(a)      Question                                                                      (2)
          Calculate Japan's rate of inflation between 2022 and 2023.
          Answer / Indicative Content
                                   AO2 2 marks
          Award 1 mark for correct method (% change):
          (104 − 100) ÷ 100 × 100 (1)

          Award 1 mark for the correct answer:
          = 4% (1)

          Award 2 marks if the correct answer of 4% is given even without
          working.
          Award 1 mark if % symbol is missing.
          Do not award marks for the formula alone.


4(b)      Question                                                                      (6)
          Analyse the possible effects on Japanese households of raising
          interest rates.
          Indicative Content · AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks)

          AO2 and AO3
            • Interest rates are the cost of borrowing and the reward for
                saving.
              •   Raising rates from −0.1% to 0.1% makes borrowing more
                  expensive for Japanese households · mortgage repayments
                  and loan costs rise.
              •   This reduces disposable income for households with
                  variable-rate debts, leaving them less to spend on other
                  goods and services.
              •   Savers benefit from higher returns on bank deposits, which is
                  particularly important for Japan's ageing population relying on
                  savings income.
              •   Higher interest rates may strengthen the yen, making
                  imported goods cheaper for Japanese households but
                  exports less competitive.
              •   Reduced consumer spending may slow economic growth,
                  potentially affecting household job security and wage growth.
              •   Higher rates discourage new borrowing, so households may
                  delay major purchases such as homes and cars.

                             be excellent, with a thorough analysis of issues (AO3).


4(c)      Question                                                                  (12)
          Evaluate the use of government subsidies to support the renewable
          energy industry in a country such as Japan.
          Indicative Content · AO2 (4 marks) / AO3 (4 marks) / AO4 (4
          marks)

          AO2 and AO3 · advantages of subsidies
            • A subsidy is a government payment to suppliers to reduce
                their costs of production.
             •   Subsidies of ¥20 trillion lower production costs for solar
                 panel, wind turbine and EV battery producers, shifting supply
                 to the right.
             •   Lower production costs translate into lower prices for
                 renewable energy, making it more affordable and accessible
                 for Japanese households and firms.
             •   Subsidies help Japan achieve its target of carbon neutrality
                 by 2050 by reducing emissions and combating climate
                 change.
             •   Supporting a new industry creates jobs in manufacturing,
                 installation and research · reducing unemployment and
                 boosting economic growth.
             •   Investment in green technology builds comparative
                 advantage for Japan, potentially creating future exports and
                 reducing dependence on imported fossil fuels.
             •   Positive externalities of production are generated · less air
                 pollution, better public health, and a more sustainable
                 economy.


          AO4 · disadvantages / evaluation
            • ¥20 trillion is an opportunity cost: the money could have been
               spent on healthcare, education or pensions, which may be
               needed more in an ageing society.
             •   Subsidies may lead to inefficiency · firms may rely on
                  government support rather than innovating, and the most
                  efficient firms may not receive subsidies.
              •   Taxpayers ultimately fund the subsidies, so they raise the tax
                  burden or add to government debt.
              •   There is a risk of over-production or 'picking winners'
                  incorrectly if the government supports technologies that do
                  not become commercially viable.
              •   Other countries (EU, US, China) could retaliate with their own
                  subsidies, leading to costly subsidy wars.
              •   Market-based alternatives such as carbon taxes or emissions
                  trading may be more efficient in reducing emissions.
              •   Overall judgement: subsidies can be effective if well-targeted
                  and time-limited, but depend on government setting clear
                  conditions, choosing the right technologies, and monitoring
                  outcomes carefully.


Level     Mark         Descriptor

          0               •   No rewardable material.

Level 1   1–4             •   Demonstrates isolated elements of relevant knowledge and
                              understanding; may lack development of relevant points.
                              Limited application of economic terms, concepts, theories
                              and calculations (AO2).
                          •   Information presented will lack selectivity and organisation.
                              Chain of reasoning may be attempted. Interpretation of
                              economic information will be limited, with a lack of analysis of
                              issues (AO3).
                          •   Only offers one viewpoint, meaning there is an unbalanced
                              and incomplete evaluation, showing limited understanding
                              and awareness. A judgement or conclusion is unlikely to be
                              attempted (AO4).

Level 2   5–8             •   Demonstrates partial selectivity and some accurate
                              knowledge and understanding by developing some relevant
                              points. Partial application of economic terms, concepts,
                              theories and calculations (AO2).
                          •   Information presented will demonstrate some selectivity and
                              organisation. Chain of reasoning will be present but may be
                              limited. Interpretation of economic information will be good,
                              with some analysis of issues (AO3).
                          •   Offers more than one viewpoint but the argument may lack
                              balance, leading to an evaluation that may not demonstrate
                              full understanding and awareness. A judgement or
                              conclusion is attempted, but may not be fully supported
                              (AO4).

Level 3   9–12            •   Demonstrates specific and accurate knowledge and
                              understanding by developing relevant points. Appropriate
                              application of economic terms, concepts, theories and
                              calculations (AO2).
                          •   Information presented will demonstrate excellent selectivity
Level   Mark   Descriptor

                      and organisation. Chain of reasoning will be coherent and
                      logical. Interpretation of economic information will be
                      excellent with a thorough analysis of issues (AO3).
                  •   Offers more than one viewpoint. The argument is well
                      balanced and coherent, leading to an evaluation that
                      demonstrates full understanding and awareness. A supported
                      judgement or conclusion is present (AO4).
